Chem., 2017, DOI: 10.1039/C7NJ03880A. # Colorimetric Sensor of H2O2 Based on Co3O4–Montmorillonite Nanocomposites with the Peroxidase Activity Xixi Zhua, Wei Chena, Kaili Wua, Hongyu Lia, Min Fua, Qingyun Liu*a, Xiao Zhangb a College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266590, P. R. China b Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science & Technology, Qingdao 266042, China * Corresponding Author E-mail: qyliu@sdust.edu.cn; qyliusdu@163.com Tel.: +86 0532 86057757 Fax: +86 0532 80681197 ### Abstract Co3O4 nanoparticles deposited on montmorillonite (Co3O4-MMT NPs) were synthesized via a facile method and characterized by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Significantly, the as-prepared Co3O4-MMT NPs were demonstrated to possess an intrinsic peroxidase-like activity that can rapidly catalytic oxidize peroxidase substrate 3,3’,5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB·2HCl) by H2O2, accompanied with a visible color change (colorless to blue), which developed a sensitive colorimetric sensor for detecting H2O2. The catalytic reaction of Co3O4-MMT NPs followed the Michaelis-Menten kinetics equation, and the composites showed good affinity towards TMB. The proposed method can detect H2O2 in the range of 10-100 μM with a low detection limit (DL) of 8.7 μM. Fluorescent data revealed that the electron transfer among catalyst, TMB and H2O2 may contribute to the peroxidase-like activity of the Co3O4-MMT NPs. Finally, a colorimetric method for detecting H2O2 was proposed, and the robustness of the Co3O4-MMT NPs was pretty good. The sensor of H2O2 based on Co3O4-MMT NPs can be found to have great potential applications in the medical, food and environmental fields. **Key words:** Co3O4-MMT; nanocomposites; peroxidase; H2O2; colorimetric ### 1. Introduction As an important chemical substances, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has been widely used in textile industry, chemical industry, paper industry, food industry, and so on. Moreover, H2O2 is a kind of intermediate in some metabolic reaction of human bodies, including glucose oxidation, trioxypurine oxidation, acetylcholine decomposition, etc. On this basis, it is possible to detect target object (glucose, trioxypurine, and acetylcholine) though measuring the concentration of H2O2 indirectly. In addition, H2O2 also exists in some food, seawater and waste water. Thus, it is necessary to develop a convenient and facile method to determine the concentration of these micromolecules, such as H2O2 and acetylcholine [1-3]. Compared with traditional methods, such as electrochemistry [4], chemiluminescence [5], liquid chromatography [6], fluorescence [7] and bioelectrochemistry [8], the colorimetric assay attracts more attention due to its low-cost, timesaving, sensitivity and pretty simplicity [9]. Colorimetric detection technologies provide a valuable set of approaches suitable in routine tests for environmental monitoring and biomedical diagnosis purposes. As a natural peroxidases, horseradish peroxidase (HRP) can catalyze the oxidation of some substrates (ascorbate, ferrocyanide, catochrome C and the leuco form of many dyes, etc.) in the presence of H2O2 [10]. Thus, HRP is the most common enzyme for H2O2 sensing. However, the application of HRP has been limited due to the complicated process of preparation and purification, as well as easy inactivation under extreme conditions, including basic medium, strong acidic and high temperature [11]. It is amusing that the magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles possess the peroxidase-like activity, which could be used as mimic enzymes to replace HRP [12] Since then, various nanomaterials were investigated as peroxidase, such as Co3O4 NPs [13], TCPP-NiO NPs [14], Fe–Co NPs [15], CNPs [16], etc. As we know, nanoparticles are tend to aggregate, due to the relatively smaller particle size with high interface energy, resulting in the decrease of catalytic activity to some extent. Thus, some supports are necessary to prepare the nanocomposite, in order to maintain the catalytic activity of small nanoparticles. Montmorillonite (MMT) is one kind of phyllosilicate minerals composed by one layer of aluminum-oxygen octahedron and two layers of silicon-oxygen tetrahedron, and it has been widely applied in separation, catalysis, optical and electrical devices due to layered structure, large specific area and high cation exchange capacity [17]. There are many reports on MMT based materials, included inorganic materials and organic materials, such as ZnO-MMT, CeO2-MMT, CuS-MMT, and Polyethylene-MMT etc [18-24]. From these reports, it can be found that MMT plays a key role in the dispersion of other components, and then prevents the aggregation of the nanoparticles. With these ideas in our mind, the combination of Co3O4 and MMT is attractive to construct an efficient nanocomposite as enzyme mimic. So far as we know, there is no relevant report about Co3O4-MMT NPs applied in H2O2 detection. Herein, Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites were prepared through a one-step hydrothermal route and investigated as efficient and novel peroxidase mimics. Co3O4 nanoparticles were homogeneously distributed on the MMT sheets. Co3O4-MMT NPs possessed the catalytic activity of peroxidase and weak oxidase, and showed typical Michealis-Menten kinetics and good affinity toward TMB. The peroxidase-like activity of the Co3O4-MMT NPs may originate from the electron transfer among catalyst, TMB and H2O2. The fluorescent technology was employed to verify the catalytic activities of Co3O4-MMT NPs and to support the mechanism analysis. Based on the peroxidase activity, sensitive colorimetric sensors of H2O2 was designed. ### 2. Experimental section #### 2.1 Reagents Co(NO3)2·6H2O, acetic acid (HAc), hydrogen peroxide (30% wt%, H2O2) and sodium acetate (NaAc)were obtained from Beijingchemical Reagent Factory (Beijing, China). 3,3,5,5-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and Montmorillonite (MMT) were obtained from Solarbio (Tianjin, China). Terephthalic acid (TA) was obtained from Chengdu cologne chemical reagent factory. The chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade. All solutions were prepared with De-ionized water. #### 2.2 Characterization The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained using a Rigaku D/Max2500PC diffractometer with graphite monochromatized Cu K$\alpha$ radiation. The elemental compositions of the composites were identified using energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The morphology and shape of the nanocomposites were imaged by the transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a Hitachi UH4150 spectrophotometer (Japan). Fluorometric measurement was carried out by a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan). #### 2.3 Preparation of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites were prepared as follows: Firstly, 0.3000g MMT was dissolved in 13mL alcohol aqueous solution (H2O2:C2H5OH=1:1) under stirring violently for 18h to form a homogeneous suspension. Secondly, 0.2910g of the Co(NO3)2 (1mmol) were added into the above suspension under vigorous stirring for another 2h. The resulting solution was assigned as solution A. Thirdly, 1.25 ml ammonium hydroxide was cautiously dropwise added into the solution A and stirred for 1h. Subsequently, the above solution was sealed in an autoclave and heated at 170 °C for 8h. Finally, the products were collected by centrifugation, washed with distilled water for three times and subsequently ethanol, and then dried at 60 °C for 8 h in air. #### 2.4 Peroxidase-like activity of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites The peroxidase-like activity of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites was investigated via the results of catalytic oxidizing the substrate TMB by H2O2. In a typical procedure, 0.2 mL of TMB (1 mM) in sodium acetate buffer solution (pH=4.0) was dealt with different reaction systems, listed in Table 1. The total reaction volume was 2 mL. The absorption spectra of different experimental systems were measured by recording the absorption spectra in wavelength mode (500-800nm). **Table 1** Different reaction systems.
Number Different reaction systems
a 0.2 mL Co3O4-MMT (0.3 mg mL−1) + 0.2 mL H2O2 (0.25 M)
b 0.2 mL Co3O4-MMT (0.3 mg mL−1)
c 0.2 mL MMT (0.3 mg mL−1) + 0.2 mL H2O2 (0.25 M)
d 0.2 mL Co3O4 NPs (0.3 mg mL−1) + 0.2 mL H2O2 (0.25 M)
e 0.2 mL H2O2 (0.25 M)
f Nothing
#### 2.5 Kinetics measurements The kinetic measurement of the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites was executed on a UV-vis absorbance spectrophotometer under time-scan mode. The experiment process was as follows: 0.3 mg mL−1 Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites as enzyme mimics in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH=4.0) with 0.2 mL of TMB (1mM) and 0.2 mL of H2O2 (10–1000 mM) as a substrate, or 0.2 mL of H2O2 (0.25 M) and 0.2 mL of TMB (0.1–1 mM) as a substrate, and keep total reaction volume was 2mL. The Michaelis–Menten constant (Km) could be obtained according to the Lineweaver-Burk plot: $1/v = K_m/V_{max} (1/[S] + 1/K_m)$, where v, Vmax, [S] and Km is represent the initial velocity, the maximal reaction velocity, the concentration of the substrate, and the Michaelis constant, respectively [25]. #### 2.6 Detection of H2O2 using Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites The experiments of colorimetric detection of H2O2 with Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites as catalysts were performed as follows: using TMB (0.2 mL, 1 mM) as peroxidase substrate in the presence of H2O2 (0.2 mL) and 0.2 mL Co3O4-MMT NPs (0.3 mg·mL−1), together with 1.4 mL sodium acetate buffer solution (pH=4.0). The catalytic reaction of TMB oxidized by H2O2 in the presence of Co3O4-MMT NPs was investigated according to the absorbance of the resulting solution. ### 3. Results and discussion #### 3.1 Characterization ![XRD spectra](XRD_spectra.png) **Fig. 1.** The XRD spectra of MMT (a), Co3O4 NPs (b) and Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites (c), respectively. The XRD patterns of MMT, Co3O4, and Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites were shown in Fig.1. Diffraction peak at 7.21° in Fig.1a was the characteristic peak of MMT correspond to (001) plane. From fig.1b, diffraction peaks at 19.04°, 31.32°, 36.89°, and 44.87° corresponded to the (1 1 1), (2 2 0), (3 1 1) and (4 0 0) planes) respectively, which agreed well with reported Co3O4 system (JCPDS card no.42-1467) [26], thus demonstrating the existence of the crystalline Co3O4. From the Fig.1c, besides the above characteristic peaks of Co3O4, the peak appears at 7.89° can be indexed as (001) plane of MMT, suggesting that Co3O4 nanoparticles were deposited on the surface of MMT, which can be observed in the subsequent TEM images. ![TEM images](TEM_images.png) **Fig. 2.** TEM images of MMT (A), Co3O4 NPs (B) and Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites (C), respectively. The morphologies of the MMT, Co3O4 NPs and Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites were imaged by TEM, shown in Fig. 2. It can be seen from Fig. 2b that Co3O4 nanoparticles without MMT as support easily aggregated. However, as shown in Fig. 2c, the Co3O4 nanoparticles with ca. 10-20 nm in size were dispersed well on the surface of MMT sheets, suggesting that MMT was an ideal support for the growth of Co3O4 nanoparticles. In order to determine the influence of the reaction time on the preparation of nanocomposites, we have carried out the experiments for different reaction time (2h, 4h, 6h, 8h, 10h). And the obtained nanocomposites were characterized by XRD and SEM, shown in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2 (supporting information), respectively. From the XRD patterns, it can be seen that Co3O4 nanoparticles have been formed after reaction for 2h. The diffraction peaks at 19.04°, 31.32°, 36.89°, and 44.87° agreed well with that of peak of Co3O4 (JCPDS card no.42-1467). Moreover, from the XRD data, it can be found that the longer the experimental time, the more the intensity of the peaks of Co3O4 nanoparticles. It can be found that when experiments have carried out for 8 h, the intensity of diffraction peaks of Co3O4 nanoparticles have reached maximum. However, it can be also found that the XRD peaks of MMT is hardly any changed. From SEM images shown in Fig S2 (supporting information), it can be seen that nanocomposites, which have been prepared under the condition of reaction for 8 h, dispersed well on the surface of MMT. Nevertheless, when the reaction have sustained for 10 h, it can be found that Co3O4 nanoparticles have agglomerated on the surface of MMT to some extent. On the basis of the results described above, it can be indicated that Montmorillonite provide a good supporter for Co3O4 growing, but the influence of Montmorillonite on the XRD of the Co3O4 is very limited. ![EDS spectra](EDS_spectra.png) **Fig. 3.** Energy-dispersive X-ray spectra of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites The chemical components of the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites were further confirmed by EDS, shown in Fig. 3. It can be observed that there are two obvious Co and O peaks, strongly suggesting that Co3O4 successfully anchored onto MMT materials. The reason why the content of O was more than that of Co was that the montmorillonite also had lots of O elements. In addition, the peak of Si in the spectrum was attributed to montmorillonite. #### 3.2 Peroxidase-like activity of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites The peroxidase-like activity of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites was investigated via the results of catalytic oxidizing TMB by H2O2. TMB was oxidized into an intermediate product with an obvious color change from colorless to blue corresponding to a strong absorption band at 652 nm, and this process could be observed by the naked eyes. From Fig. 4, it can be seen that the absorbance of system (a) is higher than that of other systems, indicating that Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites possessed the enhanced the peroxidase catalytic activity. It also can be found that Co3O4 nanoparticles (b) possessed the peroxidase activity, which is in accordance with the previous report [13], weaker than that of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites, suggesting that MMT as a good supporter played an important role in dispersion of Co3O4 nanoparticles. That is to say, MMT increases the dispersity of Co3O4 nanoparticles, and the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites had more active sites with substrate than that of sole Co3O4 nanoparticles. By contrast, the experimental system without Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites (d) exhibited a negligible color change. It can be concluded from system (e) that sole MMT was not demonstrated the peroxidase activity. System (f) shows no obvious color change, indicating that Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites are required for the catalytic oxidation reaction. Interestingly, system (c) demonstrated a wathet blue change, indicating the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites possessed weak oxidase-like activity. Overall, Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites exhibited peroxidase- and oxidase-like catalytic activity under identical conditions. It is the di-enzymes effects that accelerate the oxidation of TMB and result in the rapid response for H2O2. Subsequently, we emphasized to study the peroxidase-like properties of Co3O4-MMT, because the oxidase-like activity is relatively weak. ![Absorbance spectra](Absorbance_spectra.png) **Fig. 4.** (a) TMB + Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites + H2O2, (b) TMB + Co3O4 + H2O2 (c) TMB + Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites, (d) Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites + H2O2, (e) TMB +MMT+ H2O2, (f) TMB + H2O2, and (j) TMB, respectively. Each of systems was measured by monitoring the absorbance data from 500 to 800 nm. The catalytic role of Co3O4-MMT on ABTS and pyrogallol were assessed and compared with TMB as shown in fig. S3. In the same reaction time, the ABTS and pyrogallol shows weak color changes. In contrast, the obvious blue change of the TMB is obtained. #### 3.3 Experimental condition optimization Similar to natural peroxidases and the previous reported nanomaterial-based peroxidase mimics [27-29], the catalytic activity of Co3O4-MMT NPs depends on pH and temperature, respectively. Changes of the pH (1.7-9.0) and the temperature (20 - 60°C) were executed to evaluate the influences of pH and temperature on catalytic activity of Co3O4-MMT NPs, respectively. As shown in Fig.5a, the catalytic activity of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites first increased untill pH=4.0 and then declined with pH value increases. Thus, the optimal pH was approximately 4.0, revealing that this reaction occurred easily in weak acidic solutions, which was consistent with the previous reports [14, 20]. Fig.5b shows temperature dependence of catalytic activity. The highest catalytic activity was obtained at approximately 40°C, indicating that the optimal temperature was 40°C, which was consistent with porphyrin functionalized ZnS nanoparticles [30]. ![pH and temperature influences](pH_temp_influences.png) **Fig. 5.** Influences of (A) pH and (B) temperature on the catalytic oxidation of 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 and Co3O4-MMT NPs. The maximum point in each curve (A–B) was set as 100%. #### 3.4 Kinetic studies of the peroxidase-like activity of the Co3O4-MMT NPs It is known that the catalytic oxidation of TMB by many enzymes mimetics follows the Michaelis-Menten behaviors. Michaelis-Menten equation ($v = V_{max}S/(K_m + S)$) as one of the earliest descriptions of the enzymes action, can reveal the relationship between substrate concentration (S) and initial rate (v). Series of assays were carried out with the change of one substrate concentration, and kept the other conditions fixed at the same time, so as to further inveatigate the peroxidase-like activity of Co3O4-MMT NPs. Typical Michaels-Menten curves of TMB and H2O2 were obtained by plotting concentration against the initial reaction rate, shown in Fig. 6. ![Kinetic analysis](Kinetic_analysis.png) **Fig. 6.** Steady-state kinetic analysis utilizing the Michaelis-Menten model (A, B) and Lineweaver–Burk double-reciprocal model (C, D) for Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites. The concentration of H2O2 was 0.25 M while TMB concentration was altered (A, C). In turn, the concentration of TMB was 1 mM and H2O2 concentration was varied (B, D). Michaelis constant (Km) is often related to the affinity between the substrate and the catalyst obtained by the Lineweaver-Burk (L-B) plot. The smaller the value of Km, the stronger the affinity between the substrate and the catalysts. Comparison of maximum reaction rate (Vmax) and the apparent Michealis constant (Km) between Co3O4-MMT NPs and HRP were illustrated in Table 2. It can be seen that the Km value for Co3O4-MMT NPs with TMB as the substrate is calculated to be 0.002163 mM, which was 20 times lower than that for HRP enzyme, indicating that the stronger affinity of Co3O4-MMT NPs towards TMB than that of HRP. However, the Km value of Co3O4-MMT NPs with H2O2 as substrate is 20.492 mM, higher than that of HRP. **Table 2** Comparison of the apparent Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and the maximum reaction velocity (Vmax).
Catalyst Km [mM] Vmax [10-8Ms-1] Reference
H2O2 TMB H2O2 TMB
HRP 3.7 0.434 8.71 10.00 [13]
Co3O4-MMT 20.492 0.002163 34.2349 1.74948 This work
PDI-Co3O4 34.38 0.0562 0.3294 11130 [31]
Co3O4 NPs 140.07 0.037 12.1 6.27 [13]
H2TCPP-Co3O4 6.10 0.0283 0.708 0.64 [32]
#### 3.5 Mechanism of the peroxidase-like activity of Co3O4-MMT NPs In order to figure out the catalytic mechanism of Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites, a series of experiments were carried out. In these experiments, terephthalic acid was employed as a fluorescence probe to replace TMB and estimate the effects of the Co3O4-MMT NPs on [·OH] radicals. Terephthalic acid easily captured [·OH] radicals to form 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid which can generate high fluorescent intensity [33]. The typical assay was performed using 10mM H2O2, 0.5mM terephthalic acid and the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites with different concentration were incubated in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH=4.0, 100 mM) at 40 °C for 40 min. The fluorescent results were displayed in Fig. 7. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the amount of the Co3O4-MMT NPs increased, which was inconsistent with that of some previous works [14, 30]. In other words, the higher concentration of the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites, the less [·OH] radicals produced, suggesting that the composites inhibit the production of [·OH] radicals. According to the relevant publications [34-35], this kind of efficient catalytic performance may originate from the electron transfer among catalyst, TMB and H2O2. On the basis of experimental results and relevant reports, we proposed the catalytic mechanism of the Co3O4-MMT composites, illustrated in Scheme 1. The higher catalytic activity of Co3O4-MMT composites may be explained due to transfer of electrons from the top of the valence band of Co3O4-MMT composites to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of H2O2. Moreover, the amino groups of TMB donate lone-pair electrons to Co3O4-MMT composites, which confers an increase in electron density and mobility in Co3O4-MMT composites. This transfer of charges increases the electro-chemical potential from the LUMO of H2O2 and therefore helps the electron transfer process from Co3O4-MMT composites to H2O2 [36]. ![Fluorescence intensity](Fluorescence_intensity.png) **Fig. 7.** The influence of the Co3O4-MMT NPs on the generation of hydroxyl radical with terephthalic acid (TA) as a fluorescent probe. Reaction conditions: TA (0.35 mM), H2O2 (0.25 M), and the Co3O4-MMT NPs with different concentration (0–700 g mL-1) as the curves indicated (a–h) were incubated in acetate buffer (pH 4.0, 100 mM) at 40 °C for 40 min. ![Scheme 1](Scheme_1.png) **Scheme 1.** Schematic illustration of colorimetric detection of H2O2 catalyzed by using Co3O4-MMT NPs. #### 3.6 Detection of H2O2 and using Co3O4-MMT NPs According to the above results, it can be inferred that the Co3O4-MMT NPs possessed intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, which can catalytic oxidize TMB by H2O2 to obtain the blue oxTMB. Therefore, a simple colorimetric method for detecting H2O2 was developed. Under the optimum experimental conditions, the absorbance at 652 nm versus concentrations of H2O2 response curve was plotted in Fig. 8A. The absorbance gradually increased with the increasing of H2O2 concentration ranged from 2 to 900 μM. The corresponding linear calibration plot was shown in Fig. 8b, revealing a good linear relationship in the range of 10-100 μM with a detection limit of 8.7μM, slightly lower than that of pure Co3O4 NPs (10 μM) [13]. Furthermore, the visualized variation could be observed by the naked eye, which represents the blue becomes darker and darker with the increasing concentration of the H2O2, as shown in the inset of Fig. 8b. ![Dose-response curve](Dose_response_curve.png) **Fig. 8.** (A) A dose-response curve depending of the absorbance at 652 nm on the concentration of H2O2 from 2μM to 900 μM. (B) The corresponding linear calibration plot, in which, error bars represent the standard error originated from three repeated measurements. Inset: photographs of colored products at different concentration of H2O2. **Table 3** Comparison of the response of other H2O2 sensors.
Catalyst Detection limit (μM) Linear range (μM) Reference
Co3O4-MMT 8.7 10-100 This work
PDI-Co3O4 2.77 5-100 [31]
Co3O4 NPs 10 50-25000 [13]
H2TCPP-Co3O4 0.4 1-75 [32]
#### 3.7 Application to real sample analysis In modern society, contact lenses has been increasingly used, and in the contact lens industry, H2O2 is often adopted in the disinfection process [37-38]. However, H2O2 is harmful to human eyes because of its oxidatice properties. Therefore, it is important to determine the content of H2O2 in the contact lens. As mentioned above, colorimetric method for detecting H2O2 was proposed based on Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites, and a series of assays were performed to confirm the content of H2O2 in the contact lens based on our proposed method under the above optimal conditions following. Moreover, the standard addition method was adopted in these assays under the same conditions for three times. The lens care solutions were spiked with different concentration of H2O2, and the results were listed in Table 4. There are no obvious differences between spiked and values obtained by the calibration curve (Fig. 8b). Moreover, the recovery of H2O2 in four lens care solution samples is in the range of 102 - 106.7% and the relative standard deviation (RSD) values were as low as 0.39%, indicating that this colorimetric H2O2 sensor based on our proposed method is feasible in real simple analysis. **Table 4** Determination of H2O2 in lens care solution in the presence of Co3O4-MMT NPs
samples Added (μM) Found (μM) Recovery (%) RSD (%)
1 20 22.4 103 0.43
2 40 42.2 105.5 0.39
3 60 61.2 102 0.57
4 80 85.4 106.7 0.96
#### 3.8 Robustness of peroxidase activity of Co3O4-MMT NPs As we know, the stability of the catalyst is of great importance for the following application. The temperature and pH are the most common impact factors on the structure and catalytic activity of catalysts. According to the reference [12], the catalytic activity of the HRP dramatically decreased after being treated at different temperatures and pH values for 2 hours. Based on the similar method, the stability of Co3O4-MMT NPs were studied in this paper. The activity of the Co3O4-MMT NPs were measured under optimum conditions (pH 4.0, 40 °C) after being incubated at different pH values (2-10) and temperatures (0-80 °C) for 2 h, shown in Fig. 9 and Fig. 10, respectively. From the data, it can be seen that there are no obvious changes of Co3O4-MMT activity after incubating in different pH values and temperatures, and the lowest relative activitiy are exceed 90%, indicating that the Co3O4-MMT NPs possesses good stability under a widely range of pH values form 1 to 10 and temperatures from 0 to 80 °C, respectively. ![pH stability](pH_stability.png) **Fig. 9.** Relative catalytic activity of the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites after incubation at a range of values of pH (1.0–10.0) for 2 h. ![Temperature stability](Temp_stability.png) **Fig. 10.** Relative catalytic activity of the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites after incubation at a range of temperatures (0–80 °C) for 2 h. Furthermore, the reusability of the catalysts is also very crucial. In order to figure out the reusability of Co3O4-MMT NPs, a series of experiments were performed to examine the catalytic activity in different cycles. From Fig. 14, it can be concluded that only a slightly decline of relative activity was observed after 5 cycles. In conclusion, the robustness of the Co3O4-MMT NPs is pretty good and it can be used in the practical applications under various fields. ![Reusability](Reusability.png) **Fig. 11.** Relative catalytic activity of the Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites in five successive recycling catalysis. ### 4. Conclusion In summary, this work reported a facile method to synthesize Co3O4-MMT nanocomposites by one-step hydrothermal route, and this nanoparticles were demonstrated to possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity which could catalytic oxidize TMB by H2O2 accompanied with visible color change by naked eyes. The Co3O4-MMT NPs exhibited higher catalytic activity than pure MMT and the catalytic capacity was strongly dependent on temperature and pH. The results showed that the catalysis followed typical Michaelis-Menten kinetics and had a strong affinity to TMB. This kind of efficient catalytic performance may derive from the electron transfer among catalyst, TMB and H2O2. Based on the results described above, a simple, colorimetric and visual method for detecting H2O2 was established, which had a promising application in food detection, biochemical analysis and environmental monitoring. ![Final Schematic](Final_Schematic.png) Schematic illustration of colorimetric detection of H2O2 catalyzed by using Co3O4-MMT NPs.

# Soft template induced phase selective synthesis of Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> nanomagnets: one step towards peroxidase-mimic activity allowing colorimetric sensing of thioglycolic acid†

Anindita Roy, Ramkrishna Sahoo, Chaiti Ray, Soumen Dutta and Tarasankar Pal*

Different Schiff's base (SB) templates have been obtained to mediate phase selective synthesis of $\alpha$- or $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> from the same temperature condition. The inherent phase transformation tendency ($\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> to $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>) is found to be inhibited. Again, the phase selective evolution emerges out of the binding modes of SBs which has been illustrated here. The nature of SBs governs the shape and size distribution of Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> NPs. As both the phases are magnetically active, easy magnetic separation of them widens their applicability in catalysis. Magnetically active both $\alpha$- and $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> mimic interesting peroxidase like activity by oxidising colourless 3,3',5,5'-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue coloured oxidised product (Ox-TMB) in aqueous H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>. These results prompted us to improvise further to detect thioglycolic acid (TGA) at a micromolar level which provides commercial applicability.

## Introduction

Iron oxides occur ubiquitously in environmental, geological, planetary, and technological settings. Amongst the polymorphs of ferric oxides, hematite ($\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>) is the most stable form and being an n-type semiconductor, it attracts a great deal of attention for water treatment, catalysis, electrocatalysis, energy storage and gas sensing.<sup>1</sup> However; the antiferromagnetic (AF) nature of $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> restricts the application pertaining to its separation with an external magnet. The importance of a magnetically separable catalyst finds innumerable applications in biology, radiology, therapeutics, electronics etc.<sup>2</sup> Hence, there comes the usage of maghemite i.e. $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> in different fields. Being cation-deficient spinel ($Fd\bar{3}m$), $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> shows ferromagnetism and the charge neutrality is rendered by cation vacancies, with a Curie temperature ($T_C$) of 928 K. Due to canted AF property, rhombohedral $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> ($R\bar{3}c$), below ~260 K (Morin temperature-$T_M$), shows weak ferromagnetism (WF) and that is explained by the Dzyaloshinsky<sup>3</sup>–Moriya<sup>4</sup> mechanism ($T_C$ 950 K). Tailoring of magnetic property of nanocrystals relates to well-defined size, shape and self-assembly which has become a topic of intensive research because of the enormous scope for applications of these materials as storage media, biomedical devices, optical devices, sensors and magnetic fluids. Transformation of magnetically active meta-stable $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> into the most stable magnetically inactive $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> is a consequence of temperature, time domain and precursor salt variations.<sup>5</sup> Now-a-days, template assisted synthesis has become very popular for obtaining monodispersed crystalline iron oxide nanoparticles (NPs). Among these, carbon NPs,<sup>6</sup> carbon nanotubes (CNTs),<sup>7</sup> mesoporous silica<sup>8</sup> etc. are successfully employed as the well suited templates. Self-aggregation of NPs can be prevented in the templates due to compartmentalization of the particles within the restricted domain of the template cavity.

In case of hard template mediated multistep synthesis,<sup>5</sup> Ohkoshi *et al.* showed that template cavity helps the oxidation of the precursor under confinement at high temperature (~600–1200 °C) and subsequent template dissolution finally set free the desired oxides. In this regard, iron complexes like acetylacetonates, acetates, oleates,<sup>2</sup> iron containing different metal–organic framework, ferritin proteins etc. are used extensively as template<sup>2</sup> which upon controlled pyrolysis produce template free magnetic NPs. Using specially designed three-dimensional mesoporous silica (KIT-6), Bruce *et al.*<sup>9</sup> showed the synthesis of different mesoporous iron oxides at different reaction conditions (150–650 °C). A conceptually new strategy has been adopted by Wei *et al.*<sup>10</sup> for growing atomically thin $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> nanomaterial by bringing in transition metal oxide (TMO) sheet as host material. Again, biological template assisted synthesis of iron oxide NPs has also found their relevance towards synthesis as well as biocompatibility.<sup>2</sup>

Herein, understanding the great utility of template assisted synthesis, Schiff base (SB), a condensation product of aldehyde and amine, draws our attention to improvise a prospective template. Considering the donor–acceptor property of SB, iron(II/III) chelation is evident and then NPs stabilization is realized in the subsequent steps. Thus, four different SBs, generated from the permutative combination of two different aldehydes (glyoxal and glutaraldehyde) and two different amines (ethylenediamine and triethylenetetramine) have been employed to serve the purpose of template for the synthesis of morphologically different iron oxide. Mohr's salt was used as iron source and hydrazine as hydrolyzing reagent (Scheme 1) at a refluxing temperature of 373 K. Under this condition, morphologically different Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> NPs were produced in 1 h time as a consequence of dehydration.<sup>11</sup>

The as-synthesised magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) exhibit property as peroxidase-mimicking agent catalysing the oxidation of 3,3',5,5'-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue coloured oxidised product (Ox-TMB) in presence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> which has similar activity of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), a natural enzyme. Moreover, these enzyme-mimicking nanomaterials or nanozymes show several advantages over natural enzymes, such as cost effectiveness, tuneable catalytic activities, and high stability against rigorous conditions, thus being used in various ways and means.<sup>12,13</sup> Utility of Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub> as magnetic peroxidase-like nanozyme follows a well known pathway considering Fenton's catalysis.<sup>14</sup> But Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> lacks this ability due to insignificant proportion of Fe<sup>2+</sup> ions at the NPs surface. Here the SB template mediated growth of Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> MNPs leaves a higher propensity of Fe<sup>2+</sup> ions at the surface transcending it into an effective peroxidase-like nanozyme. Meanwhile, because of the magnetic nature the nanozyme can easily be separated, concentrated and thus reusability of MNPs also popularise the system towards TMB and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> detection. In general, to evade the sophisticated instrumentation and naked eye detection of different analytes, the colorimetric detection technique is well accepted.<sup>15–19</sup> So, we further extent our TMB oxidation catalysis on colorimetric sensing of thioglycolic acid (TGA) applying the peroxidase mimicking property of Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>.

## Experimental section

Materials used and instrumentation are described in ESI as S1 and S2† respectively.

### Synthesis

Well defined $\alpha$- and $\gamma$-phases of Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> NPs were synthesized by impregnation of Fe<sup>2+</sup> precursor; Mohr's salt into SB templates followed by hydrolysis of iron-SB templates using hydrazine as hydrolyzing agent. The general strategy can be described as follows. An aliquot of the chosen aldehyde and amine ($5 \times 10^{-2}$ M) were mixed together in 10 ml of ethanol. The mixture was stirred for ~1 h using a magnetic stirrer to produce SB template (Fig. S2a:† FTIR gives the evidence of prepared Schiff base). After 1 h of stirring, 10 ml of ethanolic solution was mixed with 90 ml aqueous Mohr's salt solution ($5 \times 10^{-2}$ M) to obtain an effective reaction mixture. Then 0.5 ml hydrazine hydrate (0.16 M) was added into the reaction mixture and the solution was refluxed, until (~1 h) the supernatant became colourless. TemFe A (from glyoxal and ethylenediamine), TemFe B (from glutaraldehyde and ethylenediamine), TemFe C (from glyoxal and triethylenetetramine) and TemFe D (from glutaraldehyde and triethylenetetramine) were prepared by the same procedure. After the preparation of four sets, the products were thoroughly washed with ethanol, water and finally separated simply by employing an external door magnet. Thorough washing of the product removed all the adhering remnants from the surface making it pure enough for further characterisation and application in catalysis.

### Catalytic oxidation of TMB

The peroxidase-like catalytic activities of both $\alpha$- or $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> are described out of the oxidation of TMB in presence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>. In this study, 25 $\mu$l ($10^{-2}$ M) ethanolic solution of TMB was oxidised by 100 $\mu$l (30%) H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> in presence of 0.1 mg of dispersed catalyst in acetate buffer medium (pH = 4.0). The steady state kinetic study has been carried out at room temperature (RT). The UV-vis spectra have been recorded at 5 minute interval at RT for performing the kinetic study. With completion of the reaction, colourless TMB solution has been found to be oxidised to blue coloured oxidised product Ox-TMB which generated a broad peak at 652 nm. Thus, formation of Ox-TMB leads to study kinetically following spectroscopic observation. The Michaelis–Menten parameter has been determined from double reciprocal Lineweaver–Burk plot.

$$1/V_0 = 1/V_{max} + K_m/V_{max}[S_0] \tag{1}$$

where $V_0$ and $V_{max}$ are initial and maximum velocity respectively, $[S_0]$ is substrate concentration, and $K_m$ represents Michaelis–Menten constant. We have carried out TMB oxidation along with the variation in TMB concentration (0–0.32 mM) and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> concentration (0–80 mM). We have also performed a control experiment of TMB oxidation in presence of SB templates only. But absence of any peak at 652 nm (Fig. S8b†) ensures the catalytic inability of SB templates for TMB oxidation catalysis.

### Detection of thioglycolic acid (TGA) concentration

In a general procedure, freshly prepared aqueous thioglycolic acid (TGA) solution (0.033–0.67 mM) was introduced into the previously mentioned reaction mixture containing TMB. Keeping other reagents unaltered, we only varied the TGA solution in the reaction mixture maintaining the final volume (3 ml) constant. Successive colour variation, arising from the concentration gradient of TGA solution, was studied thoroughly by UV-vis spectral measurements.

## Results and discussion

The crystal structure, shape, size and size distribution of NPs is the resultant effect of controlled crystallisation process. The theoretical approach to understand the mechanism of crystal formation has been explained. Lowering the high Gibbs' free energy in supersaturated solution thermodynamically favours the formation of nucleation and growth of crystal. The Gibbs' free energy per unit volume of the solid phase ($\Delta G_v$) is dependent on the concentration of the solute and is represented as,

$$\Delta G_v = -\frac{kT}{\Omega} \ln \left( \frac{C}{C_0} \right) = -\frac{kT}{\Omega} \ln(1 + \sigma) \tag{2}$$

where, $C$ and $C_0$ are the concentrations of the solute and the equilibrium concentration or solubility respectively, $\sigma$ is supersaturation concentration (defined by $(C - C_0)/C_0$) and $\Omega$ is the atomic volume. So, for spontaneous nucleation, the solute concentration must exceed than that of equilibrium in reaction medium. Again the nucleus becomes stable only when the radius size exceeds the critical size $r^*$; which can be explained by

$$r^* = -2\gamma/\Delta G_v \tag{3}$$

where $\gamma$ denotes surface energy per unit area. If the nucleus radius is less than $r^*$ then it will solubilise in medium; whereas for greater value of radius, it will be stable enough to grow bigger. When the particle radius is little smaller than $r^*$ then the crystallisation process become faster, forming larger particles due to more negative Gibbs' free energy of crystallisation. During the growth of particles, there is a tendency of smaller particle to coalesce together for bigger particles, bringing agglomerated products.

Nucleation is the key in progress of crystallisation.<sup>20</sup> Under refluxing condition, large numbers of nuclei are formed and remain homogeneously distributed throughout the bulk solution. Cao *et al.* also enriched the crystallisation mechanism by proposing the stronger effect of bubble formed during this refluxing condition in nucleation process, thus crystal formation takes place.<sup>21</sup> So, refluxing condition leads to high-yielding route to synthesis NPs. In our case, the refluxing condition for an hour and the N<sub>2</sub> bubble formed from the decomposition of hydrazine effects the crystallisation process by formation of large number of nucleation sites in the reaction medium. This facile synthesis leads to gram level production of phase pure crystalline $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> or $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>, depending upon the variable SB templates' nature. Oxidation of Fe<sup>2+</sup> to Fe<sup>3+</sup> is a spontaneous process in the ambient condition. Considering Mohr's salt as precursor, we wished to control the transformation of Fe<sup>2+</sup> ions, which further act as catalyst showing peroxidase-like activity. The effect of SB templates in the phase specific formation of Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> has been elaborated here. Advantageous imine group in SBs accelerates the hydrolysis of the metal hydroxide.<sup>22</sup> This supports the benefit of SB template for the synthesis of Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> nanoparticles under refluxing condition. The PXRD patterns of TemFe A, B, C (see ESI, PXRD pattern of TemFe B and C; Fig. S1†) were indexed to $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> justified with JCPDS file no. 04-0755. For TemFe D, the PXRD pattern suggests it as $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>, supported by JCPDS file no. 84-0311 (Fig. 1).

The sharp peaks indicate the crystallinity of $\alpha$- or $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>. Absence of other peaks confirm the phase purity in the system. In the present investigation, we find that the nature of the SB governs the formation of specific phases along with definite size distribution of particles. The preferential adsorption of four different SBs with different iminic bonding motif could alter the surface energy and restricts the crystal growth in certain orientation by attachment of ions, introducing formation of different phases. There is a natural tendency of greater interaction of metal ion with tridentate imine ligands. Thus, the hydrolysis is accelerated locally in presence of the imine group attached to the iron ion. So, facile formation of maghemite occurs in case of TemFe A, B and C. But in case of TemFe D, due to the larger chain length of both aldehyde and amine used in SB (TemD), particle size increases which leads to the formation of most stable hematite phase. With increase in particle size preferential nucleation to form thermodynamically stable hematite phase occur due to the nanomization of the free surface energy.<sup>23</sup> Also tenacious hold of water on the surface of nanoparticle leads to the formation of hematite.<sup>24</sup> Increasing the size of template, depending upon the chain length of aldehydes and amines in the SB skeletons, the particle size increases which is confirmed from the FESEM (Fig. S3†) and TEM (Fig. 2) images. The average particle size of TemFe A and B lies within 20–50 nm, whereas for TemFe C it varies from 100–120 nm and for nanorod (TemFe D), the length is about 400–500 nm with an aspect ratio ~20. Larger available free space of the templates support surface energy driven oriented attachment<sup>25</sup> of the evolved Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> nanoparticles. Fringe spacing in Fig. 2 shows that TemFe A has plane orientation along (311) and that of TemFe D is (116) proven by presence of fringe spacing 0.25 nm and 0.16 nm respectively. The crystalline nature of $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> is also supported by the hexagonal bright spot in selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 2). Formation of rings in the SAED patterns (Fig. 2) of the $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> signifies the polycrystalline nature.

![Fig. 1](Fig_1_placeholder)
**Fig. 1** PXRD patterns of TemFe A and TemFe D, represent existence of $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> and $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> respectively.

FTIR analysis (Fig. S2b†) was performed to confirm the formation of different phases of iron oxide NPs illustrated by the vibrational modes. Generally in maghemite, being inverse spinel, half of the Fe<sup>3+</sup> ions occupy the tetrahedral interstices and remaining half of Fe<sup>3+</sup> in octahedral sites whereas in hematite each Fe<sup>3+</sup> occupy the octahedral coordination site. Vibration of Fe<sup>3+</sup> ions in tetrahedral positions was attributed to the high frequency band and low frequency band was attributed to the vibration of Fe<sup>3+</sup> ions in octahedral positions.<sup>26</sup>

According to Waldron,<sup>26</sup> the band appeared at 350–450 cm<sup>−1</sup> is attributed to the stretching vibration of Fe<sup>3+</sup>–O<sup>2−</sup> in the octahedral complexes and at 500–600 cm<sup>−1</sup> to that of bending vibrations in tetrahedral complexes. The presence of intense peak at 500–600 regions confirms the presence of Fe<sup>3+</sup> ions in tetrahedral position which supports the formation of $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> in case of TemFe A, B and C. Again absence of such intense peak at that region in TemFe D also suggests the evolution of $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>. This association of SB template together with Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> was established from the shift of imine stretching frequency (Fig. S2b†) towards lower frequency region (1556 and 1628 cm<sup>−1</sup>) which is due to the lowering in bond order in C=N because of the drifted electronic cloud towards Fe<sup>3+</sup> ions. This suggests the formation of coordination bond between imine $\pi$ electron cloud and Fe<sup>3+</sup> ion. The tenacious adsorption of SBs as protective layer stabilises the meta stable $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>. All these stretching vibrations in FTIR analysis (Fig. S2b†) are attributed to fingerprint vibrational modes of SBs, indicating their adherence at the surface of the NPs.

The XPS spectra (Fig. S4†) have been measured considering adventitious carbon (284.0 eV) as reference material. The XPS peak position at 711.0 eV (Fe2p<sub>3/2</sub>), 724.0 eV (Fe2p<sub>1/2</sub>) and 529.0 eV (O1s) confirms the presence of Fe<sup>3+</sup> and O<sup>2−</sup> in Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>. We also observed the presence of Fe<sup>2+</sup> (709.6 eV) ions in as synthesised TemFes after deconvulation of the XPS spectra. The peak area ratio of Fe<sup>2+</sup> and Fe<sup>3+</sup> has also been obtained from the deconvulated XPS peak (Fig. 3) of iron. The [Fe<sup>2+</sup>]/[Fe<sup>3+</sup>] of TemFe A, B, C and D are calculated from the peak area respectively. Thus, the surface attached Fe<sup>2+</sup> ions and their relative concentration helps to establish the mechanism and increasing order of the catalysis. The data related to deconvulated XPS peaks for TemFe A, B, C and D are given in ESI, Table S1.†

Though the [Fe<sup>2+</sup>]/[Fe<sup>3+</sup>] of TemFe B and D were similar; but the critical observation of deconvulated peak position of TemFe D denotes that the peak corresponds to Fe<sup>2+</sup> (709.0) is shifted to 710.0 eV which reflects the formation of Fe<sup>3+</sup> ion. So, the order of the catalysis is justified. Because the peak position denotes that conversion from Fe<sup>2+</sup> to Fe<sup>3+</sup> took place. So, absence of Fe<sup>2+</sup> ions lowers the catalytic activity of TemFe D as peroxidase mimic catalyst.

### Magnetic property

Presence of magnetic domain in material is responsible for magnetic property in them. As the particle size decreases to nanoregime, tinier than the magnetic domain; NPs are considered to be completely magnetised.<sup>27</sup> Because in nanoregime the multi-magnetic-domain organization is energetically unfavourable, thus particles with single-magnetic-domain are formed.<sup>28</sup> The term “superparamagnetism” is not very accurate but is commonly used to emphasis the magnetic behaviour for paramagnetic nanoparticles which arises from the coupling of several thousands of atoms in comparison to the paramagnetism of a single atom. This magnetization can reach nearly the saturation magnetization of ferromagnetic iron oxide, but in contrast to ferromagnetic iron the particles no longer show magnetic interactions after elimination of the magnetic field. According to the theory of magnetism for exhibiting superparamagnetic behaviour, required minimum NP volume ($V_P$) is

$$V_P = \frac{25Tk_B}{K} \tag{4}$$

where, $K$ is the anisotropic constant, $T$ is the room temperature (300 K) and $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant.<sup>29</sup> Thus, spherical NP size below 26 nm exhibits superparamagnetic behaviour.<sup>30</sup> This shows the control over magnetic nature by particle size variation at nanoregime. The magnetic data of all these TemFes are examined and discussed thoroughly to decipher the linear relationship between the particle size and size dependent magnetism. Early studies also established the unusual magnetic property as the intrinsic nature in polyimine-iron materials.<sup>31</sup> Fig. 4 shows the appealing hysteresis of vacuum dried four TemFe samples obtained by SQUID magnetometer with an applied magnetic field $\pm$2 T at both low (5 K) and high (300 K) temperature. The comparative magnetic property study at both temperatures is presented in Table 1.

![Fig. 2](Fig_2_placeholder)
**Fig. 2** (a1–a4) represent HRTEM images of TemFe A, B, C and D respectively; (b1–b4) represent fringe spacing and corresponding crystal planes of TemFe A, B, C and D respectively; (c1–c4) represent SAED patterns of TemFe A, B, C and D respectively representing the polycrystalline nature of the samples.

In rhombohedral hexagonal crystal of hematite, Fe<sup>3+</sup> occupy the octahedral coordination site, making it magnetically inactive, whereas in maghemite, the unequal distribution of Fe<sup>3+</sup> in octahedral and tetrahedral voids in inverse spinel structure arouse net magnetisation. The natural tendency of magnetisation in nanoparticle is less than that of their massive parent materials. The reason behind such reduction in magnetization in NPs is often attributed to the finite size effects such as surface spin disorder, spin canting as well as magnetically dead layer on the surface of synthesized NPs.<sup>32</sup> But here in nanoregime, the inherited magnetic property of $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> is enhanced by the presence of uncompensated surface spin at grain boundary region. The uncompensated surface spins can overcome the AF property of the core part, so $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> NPs can also exhibit a net magnetisation which is reported here. The concentration of unsaturated spins on the surface of NPs depends on the size and shape of NPs which is modulated by the variable SB templates.

The anisotropy factor in eqn (5) also explains the enhanced magnetic property.

$$E_a = K_aV \tag{5}$$

where, $E_a$ is anisotropy energy, $K_a$ is the anisotropy constant and $V$ is the crystal volume. So, both the volume and shape play a noticeable role in magnetism.<sup>3</sup>

In the present investigation for all synthesized NPs, magnetic aggregation is almost absent which is in agreement with the individual particle coating and the pseudo self-assembly due to the presence of the various SB templates. So, ceasing the spin alignment with variation of temperature is observed. Thus, there is no distinct change in saturation magnetisation occurs amongst any of these TemFes at 5 and 300 K respectively. Presence of greater anisotropy in both magnetic and morphological nature of nanorod than in sphere causes spin alignment to generate net magnetism. The convergent nature of zero-field-cooling (ZFC) and field-cooling (FC) curves (Fig. S7†) were obtained under 100 Oe magnetic fields within the temperature window 5–300 K.

**Fig. 3** Deconvulation of XPS spectra of Fe2p<sub>3/2</sub> for all TemFes were done. From the ratio of the deconvulated peak area of Fe<sup>2+</sup> and Fe<sup>3+</sup>, the concentration ratios have been determined. The [Fe<sup>2+</sup>]/[Fe<sup>3+</sup>] of TemFe A, B, C and D are 1.743, 1.374, 1.112, and 1.37 respectively.

![Fig. 4](Fig_4_placeholder)
**Fig. 4** Comparison of saturation magnetization ($M_S$) data from M – H curves of as-synthesised TemFe A, B, C and D at both 5 K and 300 K.

The soft paramagnetic nature of all template-directed NPs has been explained both by M – H curve and ZFC–FC curve. Being soft magnetic materials, it is easy to align the magnetic domains in a definite direction. We observed that AF hematite (TemFe D) also shows unnatural saturation magnetisation ($M_S$) 3.74 emu g<sup>−1</sup> at RT. The WF property associated with (TemFe D) $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> can be explained by first-order transition observed at $T_M \sim 110$ °C, in Fig. S7,† which is much less than that of bulk $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> (263 K) in Dzyaloshinsky–Moriya<sup>3,4</sup> mechanisms. In this transition, the spin-flip occurs from the spin orientation along the symmetry axis to perpendicular direction with a canted angle giving rise to weak ferromagnetism. Decrease in $T_M$ with rise in temperature attributed to the anisotropy effect, lattice strain, crystal defect etc. occurred in $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>.<sup>33</sup>

The decrease in $M_S$ of $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> with temperature increase can be illustrated due to thermal fluctuations, resulting disturbance in spin alignment. But the unchanged $M_S$ of $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> can be supported by change in spin-axis from c-axis to c-plane due to increase in thermal fluctuations. There is also a possibility of self-grown oxygen vacancies, destroying the AF interactions between the sublattices,<sup>34</sup> results in net magnetism for $\alpha$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>. Increased temperature facilitates the transition of large fraction of clusters from ferromagnetic to superparamagnetic. The soft nature of the entire template stabilized Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> at RT can also be explained by the squareness value. In absence of template, the as-synthesised $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> showed as usual saturation magnetisation of ~68 and ~60 emu g<sup>−1</sup> respectively at 5 K and 300 K (Fig. S5†), which draws special attention emphasising the template mediated synthesis technique.

### Peroxidase-like activity

To observe the natural enzyme mimic property of catalyst, TMB is used as a chromogenic substance. In presence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, our as-synthesised TemFes catalyse the oxidation of TMB to produce blue-coloured Ox-TMB at RT. This crucial finding makes the system very important for analytical purposes which are enhanced by the magnetic separation technique. We have performed the catalysis with all of the TemFes and SB template free $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>. We found that amongst all catalyst variation, TemFe A act as best catalyst towards TMB oxidation reaction. This can be explained by the size variation of the catalysts. With the smallest size, the boon of high surface to volume ratio comes into the picture and the phenomenon is also true in this case, which is observed from the rate of TMB oxidation. Generally Fe<sup>2+</sup> ions are responsible for the Fenton's reaction with H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>, which here drives the catalysis of TMB oxidation. The enhanced presence of surface-attached Fe<sup>2+</sup> ions in our synthesised catalyst surface due to the SB template which is directly related with the small size of catalyst, widen the probability of catalysis. Amongst all TemFes, the order of catalysis was found; TemFe A > TemFe B > TemFe C > TemFe D (Fig. S8†).

In presence of 0.1 mg catalyst (1 mg dispersed in 1 ml H<sub>2</sub>O), 0.1 ml (30%) H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> in acetate buffer medium (pH = 4.0) efficiently oxidises (0.025 ml, $10^{−2}$ M) TMB to blue-coloured Ox-TMB generating a peak at 652 nm at RT. The oxidised TMB products become stable at acidic pH condition. The partially oxidised, one electron, TMB produces cation radical species which is in equilibrium with the diamine–diimine species forming blue coloured charge transfer complex (~652 nm), is stable at pH = 4.0. Upon increasing the pH, the stability of the spectroscopic observable peak (~652 nm) decreases and thus we observe a decrease in intensity. The mechanism of oxidation of TMB<sup>35</sup> is given in Fig. 5.

Kinetic study with the variation of both TMB concentration (80–320 $\mu$M) and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> concentration (40–80 mM) has been performed with TemFe A, relatively better catalyst than other, establishing its peroxidase-like property (Fig. 6).

### Mechanism of peroxidase-like activity

The peroxidase-like catalytic activity in TemFe may be generated from its surface-attached Fe<sup>2+</sup> ions. The mechanism may follow Fenton's reaction. The reaction can be written as (6) and (7), where $k_1$ and $k_2$ are reaction rate constants.

$$Fe^{2+} + H_2O_2 = Fe^{3+} + \cdot OH + OH^-; k_1 = 76\text{ l mol}^{-1}\text{ S}^{-1} \tag{6}$$
$$Fe^{3+} + H_2O_2 = Fe^{2+} + \cdot OOH + H^+; k_2 = 0.002\text{ l mol}^{-1}\text{ S}^{-1} \tag{7}$$

Step (7) has a low rate constant and thus become a rate limiting reaction process. So, the possible reaction mechanism can be elaborated following two cascade steps. At first, H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> get attached to the surface of the catalyst. Then Fenton's reaction takes place at the NPs surface. The Fe<sup>2+</sup> ion generates the highly active $\cdot OH$ (hydroxyl radical) species which catalyses the oxidation of TMB.<sup>36</sup> After oxidation, the blue-coloured Ox-TMB has been thoroughly investigated spectroscopically. To justify the proposed mechanistic pathway, we have also performed oxidation of terephthalic acid (TA) by H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> under same experimental condition in presence of catalyst. Though TA is a non-fluorescent compound, but reaction with highly reactive $\cdot OH$ radical, it generates 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTA) which is a highly fluorescent compound.<sup>37</sup> In presence of catalyst, we observed enhanced fluorescence due to HTA which justifies the enhanced production of $\cdot OH$ radical in presence of catalyst (Fig. S9†). Thus, $\cdot OH$ radical mediated mechanism for the oxidation of TMB with catalyst has been justified unequivocally. A direct surface attached Fe<sup>2+</sup> concentration dependent reaction mechanism for TMB oxidation catalysis can be explained explicitly from the XPS spectra (Fig. 3). The [Fe<sup>2+</sup>]/[Fe<sup>3+</sup>] ratio give the conclusive explanation of higher catalytic activity of TemFe A with respect to others when the TMB oxidation reaction goes according to Fenton's reaction as proposed. In presence of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> and TemFe catalyst in acetate buffer (pH = 4.0) medium, TMB catalytically oxidises into blue coloured Ox-TMB arising a peak at ~652 nm in 20 minutes time duration. It is important that presence of both catalyst and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> can oxidise TMB in this reaction condition. In absence of any of these, oxidation of TMB into Ox-TMB has become futile. It was also observed that rate of oxidation is dependent upon the concentration of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>. So, we can also estimate spectroscopically the concentration of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> observing the catalytic oxidation of TMB.

### Steady state kinetic study

The steady state kinetics of TMB oxidation has thoroughly done by using TemFe A as catalyst at RT keeping the concentration of one substrate constant during the variation of the concentration of other substrate. The kinetic study of this nanozymatic TMB oxidation reaction has been studied thoroughly using UV-vis spectrophotometer. Following the Lambert–Beer's law, the concentration of the Ox-TMB product with the molar extinction coefficient of 39 000 M<sup>−1</sup> cm<sup>−1</sup> is calculated from the absorbance value obtained from the UV-vis spectra with time variation. The obtained data was observed to well-fit into Michaelis–Menten equation for both the substrate, TMB and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> (Fig. S10†).

$$V = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]} \tag{8}$$

The Michaelis–Menten equation describes the relationship between the rate of substrate conversion by an enzyme and the concentration of the substrate. In this equation, $V$ is the velocity or rate of conversion, $V_{max}$ is the maximum rate of conversion, $[S]$ is the substrate concentration, and $K_m$ is the Michaelis–Menten constant.

$V_{max}$ is the direct parameter for ensuring the enzymatic catalytic activity. $K_m$ is also an important parameter in enzymatic reaction to define the binding affinity of substrate and enzyme. Lower the value of $K_m$, higher is the binding affinity of substrate towards corresponding enzyme and *vice versa*. We have also determined the $K_m$ and $V_{max}$ values of both individual substrate, H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> and TMB, considering TemFe A as enzyme mimic catalyst from double reciprocal Lineweaver–Burk plot (Fig. 6a and b). Comparison of the $K_m$ values between horseradish peroxidase (HRP)<sup>38</sup> and TemFe A has been tabled (Table 2) out.

This value helped us for discussing about peroxidase mimicking activity of as-synthesised catalyst.

The $K_m$ value calculated for TMB as substrate using TemFe A 0.0887 is much less than that of HRP 5.9. This shows the higher affinity of TemFe A towards TMB with respect to HRP, a natural enzyme. This result can be explained by the presence of more number of active sites at the surface of TemFe A whereas only one iron site is present in natural enzyme, HRP. Magnetically active TemFe A can also be separated from the reaction medium using simply an external magnet which also increases the efficiency. But we observed significantly higher $K_m$ value for H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> using TemFe A with respect to HRP. This describes that higher concentration of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> is required to achieve maximal catalytic activity. We have calculated the limit of detection (LOD) for both TMB and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> by varying TMB concentration from 0–0.32 mM and H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> concentration from 0–80 mM respectively.

### Thioglycolic acid (TGA) detection

Thioglycolic acid (TGA) or mercaptoacetic acid (MAA) has commercial importance from drug to cosmetic industries due its applications as chemical depilatory, hair perming agent etc.<sup>39</sup> Among various compounds which are applicable as relaxers for hard hair (*e.g.* cysteine, cysteamine etc.), TGA was found to possess greater efficiency in terms of reduction potential at all pH values. The chemistry behind its activity is the tendency of breaking of disulfide bond easily. The whole hardening and softening process of hair has been elaborated as follows:

$$K–S–S–K + 2R–SH \rightarrow 2K–SH + R–S–S–R \tag{9}$$
$$K–SH + O_2 \rightarrow K–S–S–K + H_2O \tag{10}$$

where K–S–S–K and K–SH denote keratin and reduced keratin respectively. Eqn (9) and (10) concisely describe the mechanism of TGA as relaxer. However, exposure of TGA present in cosmetic products towards human health has potential adverse effect.<sup>40</sup> The safety level of TGA and thioglycolate salts in cosmetic products is 5%.<sup>41</sup> Thus, the easy and effective method for detection of TGA is needed for supervising purpose. Among different detecting methods of TGA, recently employed methods are capillary electrophoresis (CE), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), ion chromatography<sup>42</sup> etc. In this study, we have introduced colorimetric detection of TGA using the peroxidase mimic property of TemFe A. Actually in presence of TGA, hindrance in the formation of blue-coloured Ox-TMB from TMB by H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> and catalyst was observed. Practically, TGA restricts the oxidising ability of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> to oxidise TMB. Increasing the amount of the TGA concentration, a linear decrease in the absorbance of Ox-TMB was observed which motivated us to go for the colorimetric detection of TGA using TMB oxidation catalysis. Observation of the change in absorbance at peak position 652 nm for Ox-TMB helps us to determine TGA quantitatively by simple UV-vis spectroscopic study. It has been demonstrated that a linear absorbance *vs.* concentration of TGA relationship can be established (Fig. 7). From the data, we have determined the limit of detection (LOD) of TGA as 50 $\mu$M ($R^2 = 0.98$).

### Mechanism

TGA has a redox active thiol side linkage which is prone to oxidation to form disulfide linkage spontaneously. In this study, the presence of TGA hinders the usual oxidation of TMB by H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> to blue coloured Ox-TMB and leads to a path for sensing TGA. The hindrance in blue colour evolution occurs due to the thermodynamically more spontaneous oxidation of TGA by H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> instead of TMB. The proclamation of feasibility of the oxidation of TGA instead of TMB by H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> is explained by the redox potential values, shown in Table 3.<sup>43</sup>

Thus, thermodynamics drives the oxidation of TGA to generate disulfide linkage by H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> in presence of TMB. The phenomenon is TGA concentration dependent.

Thus, with increase in concentration of TGA, tendency of formation of blue coloured Ox-TMB decreases. So, concentration gradient of TGA is responsible for the UV-vis spectra variation and a calibration curve can easily be obtained. Thus, leads to its application for colorimetric sensing of TGA.

## Conclusion

In conclusion, different magnetically active phase pure $\alpha$- and $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> synthesis at gram level has been illustrated without entertaining temperature change and tedious hard template dissolution strategies. The main attraction of this work lies in the fact that the synthesis of varying morphology, phase and magnetism, of all the Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> are described simply as the exposition of the nature of SB templates with variable chelating imine donor sites and chain length variation keeping other factors constant. Again SB-modified $\alpha$- and $\gamma$-Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub> driven peroxidase mimicking activity leads to the catalysis of oxidation of TMB. This fact leads to the importance of TemFes in analytical usage of colorimetric TGA sensing with much improved magnetic character. Thus, transcending the traditional barrier of templates, SBs as soft template will play an important role in near future.
